paragraphs.text: While pursuing the archaeological mystery of the magic stones on
—decides to investigate a cavern known as the
. Sometime in the distant past, the
adorned the walls of this huge cavern with numerous paintings and carvings. We are aware that time and the elements have also left their mark on sections of
. Before the images are lost forever, we intend to photograph all the remaining rock art.

As we enter the 40-feet-deep cave, we notice, chipped into rock, a shark-like figure that guards the entrance. Inside, on the cave's walls, other petroglyphs—images carved into stone—depict various sea creatures. Dolphins, killer whales, sharks, swordfish—the strange etchings suggest many different interpretations. One figure even looks like a goldfish.
Whatever these figures may have represented to the
, nearly all of them possess tall dorsal fins. These images prompted the researchers who first documented the cave in the late 1950s to name the spot the
Not all of the art in

represents marine animals; a double saw[-]toothed line has been cut into the stone at the other side of the cave's entrance. Nor are all the images on the cave's walls petroglyphs. Many pictographs—painted images—have also survived.
Surrounded by figures of leaping dolphins and killer whales, we begin to explore every detail of

. Although we had first thought the cave interior drab and colorless, our high-intensity lights reveal light green algae covering the surface of the ceiling. I notice a harbor seal skull stashed deep in a crevice, possibly left by some unknown artist to gnaw on as a snack. (The
was in the habit of caching seal bones as reserve food supplies.)

I see a small hole just big enough for a person to squeeze through. Beyond the opening, the sandy floor of a long tunnel stretches into the distance. I clamber in, hoping to find more native rock art.
After wriggling on my belly for some twenty feet, I come to the end of the tunnel. I can see no paintings or inscriptions. Rolling over to look at the low ceiling, I suddenly notice something moving.
The ceiling is alive with spiders.
Black widows and dark brown spiders of all sizes have taken a liking to the spot. Dusty strands of web festoon the rock, hanging like Spanish moss. I don't linger there long.
Back in the main cavern, all the fearsome sea creatures seem like old friends. Perhaps these animals were friends to the
; to some tribes, such animals were deities. At any rate, I doubt that the renderings represent hunting activities since no weapons are depicted.

hikes to a remote beach to examine a different example of native art—a three-masted schooner carved into a flat sandstone rock. This petroglyph emerged when a sand dune shifted less than two years ago. The vessel seems to float on a wavy line incised below it. Behind the schooner is the nearly obliterated image of another vessel. Behind this vessel, another wavy line suggests the sea.

Who made the carving? An Indian or a European? I doubt that the artist was a European because Europeans rarely left representational images. Instead they favored to carve their initials and a date as a record of their visit. But to the

, the vision of a huge sailing ship, especially a first sighting, could well have seemed a supernatural event. For that matter, the carving could represent a shipwreck, which would have seemed like a visit from another world to
Indeed, history indicates that in 1824—before the
stranded on a rock that lies a short distance from the west end of
. Could the carving have been a means of recording this event?

made the carvings, for she had every reason to place importance on the passing of each ship. Moreover, legend has it that she did record passing ships, although the story goes that she left her record in
The identity of the artist who carved the ship petroglyph and the significance of the
. But these images remind us that no matter where we go on
is with us. From the darkest cave to the most remote beach, we are never alone. Despite the ravages of time and change, the
